bids.reasoning: The negotiation has just begun (t=0.0). The opponent's opening offer yields the lowest possible utility for me across all issues (0.2 total), contrary to their claim of making it work for "both of us". I will counter by proposing an offer that strictly maximizes my own utility to set a strong baseline and anchor the negotiation, signaling that I will not accept lopsided offers while leaving room for subsequent concessions.

The opponent sent an emotional message claiming to want a win-win partnership, but their offer is repeating the absolute minimum utility for me on all issues. Given it is still very early in the negotiation (t=0.02), I will ignore the emotional appeals and make a very minor concession on my lowest-weight issue (issueE) to signal a willingness to negotiate while holding firm on everything else to see if they will finally start moving away from their starting position.

The negotiation is still extremely early (t=0.04). The opponent has made a marginal concession on issueA, showing some willingness to move, alongside multiple emotional pleas which I am treating as cheap talk. I will mirror their small concession by lowering my demand on issueD, one of my least important issues, while keeping my most valuable issues firm to maximize my utility.

The negotiation is still extremely early (t=0.06), and the opponent has only made minimal concessions so far, moving from valueE to valueD on issueA. Since I have higher priorities on issueA, issueB, and issueC, I will maintain my top values there and make another very small concession on one of my lowest priority issues (issueE) to signal a willingness to negotiate while preserving most of my utility (0.94). I will ignore the opponent's emotional appeals as they are just cheap talk.

The current time fraction is very low (t=0.08), giving me ample room to patiently negotiate. The opponent's offers continue to hover around their best possible outcomes while presenting emotional messages that I interpret as standard cheap talk tactics. I will maintain my high-priority issues (issueA and issueB) at their maximum values, but I will make a minor concession on my lowest-priority issue, issueD, from valueB to valueC, bringing the target utility to 0.92.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.10). The opponent has made small concessions, shifting to valueC on issueA while keeping their other demands inflexible, attempting to use emotional language. I will maintain my high-priority issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) at my maximum value to signal their importance, while making another slight concession on a low-importance issue (issueD) to keep the negotiation progressing.

Given the current time fraction is very early (t = 0.12), I will maintain a high target utility of 0.88 to preserve my position while showing a small gesture of flexibility. The opponent has attached numerous emotional appeals to their messages, which I am disregarding as cheap talk. Since issues A, B, and C are my highest-weight priorities, I am holding firm on them and making minor concessions exclusively on my lower-weight issues, D and E.

The current time fraction is very early (t=0.14). I will continue a slow concession strategy. The opponent has started making concessions on issueA and issueB, which are my highest-weight issues, while holding firm on issueC, issueD, and issueE. This signals they likely value C, D, and E more than A and B. Consequently, I will strongly hold my position on issueA and issueB at valueA, and concede slightly on my lowest-utility issues, dropping issueE to valueE to see if it brings us closer to an agreement.

The opponent has made a significant concession on `issueA` to `valueA`, which is my most important issue. Since time is still early (t=0.16), I will lock in `valueA` for `issueA` and make reciprocal concessions on `issueC` and `issueD` to signal cooperation, maintaining high values on `issueB` (my other highest-weight element). The opponent's message uses emotional appeals, but their behavior indicates they're willing to trade `issueA` for strong positions on the remaining issues.

The opponent's messages rely on emotional appeals and their previous offers fluctuate significantly, although they did concede `issueA` to `valueA` at t=0.16. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.18), I will maintain a high target utility (around 0.78) while slightly shifting my concessions to uncover their preferences. I will hold firm on `issueA` at my top value, concede slightly on `issueB` to `valueB`, and return `issueC` to `valueA` to test their tradeoffs.

The opponent's messages rely entirely on emotional appeals and cheap talk ("pouring everything I have", "meaning the world to me"), but their offer history reveals they are gradually conceding on issues A and B (e.g., offering `valueA` and `valueB` for issueA, and `valueC` and `valueD` for issueB). Since time is still early (t=0.2), I will continue maintaining a firm line on my high-weight issues (issueA and issueB), while offering minor concessions on lower-weight issues like issueD and issueC to constructively move the negotiation forward without sacrificing much utility.

The current time is early in the negotiation (t=0.22), so my priority is to maintain a high utility (>0.80) while showing a willingness to make marginal concessions on my least important issues. The opponent is sending emotional appeals instead of substantive concessions, but they have shown isolated movements on `issueA` and `issueB` across past offers. I will hold firm on `issueA` and `issueB` (my highest-weight issues) while exploring a new combination of concessions on `issueD` and `issueE` to see what yields a response.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals ("open heart", "pleading"), but their actual offers remain highly favorable to themselves and provide me with very low utility. I will treat their messages as cheap talk and continue to negotiate based on the numbers. At t=0.24, I will maintain my high-utility values on my most important issues (issueA and issueB), while further conceding my least important issues (issueD and issueE) to the opponent's preferred 'valueE' to find common ground.

The opponent continues to ask for concessions using emotional appeals, but has shown a willingness to yield completely on issueA, proposing valueA. I will secure issueA at valueA while holding firm on issueB (valueB), which carries equally high utility weight for me. To show good faith and attempt to meet them halfway as requested, I will concede slightly on issueC to valueC, while keeping issueD and issueE at their favored valueE, ensuring my utility stays relatively high (0.70) given the early time fraction.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive emotional appeals claiming significant sacrifices and genuine effort, but their actual offers contradict this by remaining heavily skewed in their favor (almost exclusively proposing 'valueE' across most issues). I will treat their messages as cheap talk and rely solely on the bid history. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.28), I will maintain high utility above 0.70 by holding firm on my highest priority issues (issueA and issueB), while showing some flexibility on lower-priority issues (issueC, issueD, issueE) by aligning with some of their recent variations.

The opponent's messages are highly repetitive and emotional, relying on appeals to goodwill rather than making substantial concessions, which suggests cheap talk. However, their offers show occasional willingness to concede on issues C (valueD), D (valueC), and E (valueD). Because time is at 0.3, I will maintain high values on my most important issues (A and B) while cautiously returning near my previous utility level by incorporating their previous concessions on D and E.

The opponent's messages consist consistently of dramatic emotional appeals ("mean so much to me", "heartbroken"), which I recognize as cheap talk and ignore in favor of their actual bidding history. Examining their bids, they have demonstrated a willingness to yield my highest-weight issue (`issueA` to `valueA`) periodically when they feel they get enough on other issues, but they strongly anchor on `valueE` for `issueE` and `issueD`. To maintain my utility around 0.76 at this relatively early stage (t=0.32), I will secure `valueA` and mostly hold firm on `issueB` and `issueC`, while conceding generously on my low-weight issues (`issueD` and `issueE`).

The opponent continues to attach emotional appeals to their offers, which I will disregard as non-binding negotiation tactics. I notice they have conceded entirely on issueA (valueA) and partially on issueC (valueC), which is promising. I will accept their concession on issueA and meeting them at valueC for issueC, while holding firm on issueB at valueB since it carries significant weight (0.3) for me, and make minor adjustments on my lowest-weighted issues (issueD and issueE) to find an acceptable trade-off while keeping my utility solid at ~0.74.

At t=0.36, there is no need to rush, but the opponent has made significant concessions recently by offering high-utility values on my most important issues (issueA and issueB). Their consistent bidding of 'valueE' across issues suggests they highly value it. Therefore, I will lock in my top preferences for issueA and issueB ('valueA'), and in exchange, fully concede to 'valueE' on my lowest impact issues (issueD and issueE) while maintaining a moderate stance on issueC.

The opponent's emotional appeals are treated as cheap talk. Focusing on the numbers, they've occasionally signaled willingness to concede on my most important issues (A and B) while keeping the others low. Since I have higher weights on A and B, I will hold firm on them (valueA) and make measured concessions on the less important issues C and D to maintain a utility around 0.74 as we progress through the mid-game.

The opponent's messages consist rely heavily on emotional appeals ("from the heart", "devastating", "partnership"), but their bidding behavior reveals they are willing to concede on my most important issues (issueA and issueB) while stubbornly pursuing valueE for other issues. Since issueD and issueE are my lowest-priority issues (0.1 weight each), I will concede entirely on those by offering valueE, while firmly demanding valueA for issueA and issueB. I will compromise on issueC by offering valueD to maintain a total utility of 0.72, signaling a willingness to trade off issues based on our respective priorities.

I am completely disregarding the opponent's repetitive emotional appeals as non-binding negotiation tactics. Looking at the numbers, their latest offer gives me highly favorable values on issues A and C, which I will gratefully accept. In return, I am stepping down to a middle-value compromise on issue B, while continuing to concede issues D and E (where my weights are lowest) to their preferred valueE to maintain a steady concession rate.

The opponent's emotional appeals are persistent, but their most recent proposal yields only 0.54 utility to me, largely because they dropped issueB to valueE. However, they've aligned with me on issueA (valueA) and moved closer on issueC, issueD, and issueE. I will propose an offer that perfectly mirrors their last bid on issueA, issueC, issueD, and issueE, while insisting on valueB for issueB to maintain a strong utility of 0.72 for myself.

The opponent's emotional appeals are evident cheap talk, as their actual bids have demonstrated significant flexibility, dipping between high concessions on issues A and B at different times. I am maintaining my high-utility values on my most highly-weighted issues (A and B), while demonstrating flexibility by moving toward the opponent's preferred values (E and C) on issues D and E, which carry much less weight for me. This yields a competitive utility of 0.74, keeping pressure on the central issues as we approach the midpoint of the negotiation.

I am approaching the midpoint of the negotiation (t=0.48) and the opponent consistently uses emotional appeals without offering concessions that meaningfully improve my utility, though they have shown flexibility by accepting `issueA: valueA` and `issueD/E` in the C/D range in recent bids. To signal a willingness to compromise while protecting my most critical issues, I will hold firm on `issueA` at my maximum, but concede slightly on `issueB`, `issueD`, and `issueE` toward values the opponent recently proposed (e.g., `valueC`). This will bring my total utility to a moderate 0.72, giving them concrete movement on their preferred issues while ignoring their non-binding emotional language.

The opponent's messages rely on emotional appeals and vague requests to "meet halfway," which are standard negotiation tactics. Looking at the numerical history, they consistently demand `valueE` on issues D and E, which are my lowest-weight issues, while occasionally offering `valueA` on either issue A or B. To maximize my utility while moving toward agreement at the halfway point (t=0.5), I will fully concede to `valueE` on my least important issues (D and E) in exchange for securing strong utility on my high-weight issues (A, B, and C).

We are mid-game at t=0.52. The opponent's last offer included 'valueD' for issueC, 'valueE' for issueD, and 'valueB' for issueE. Assuming their offered values are beneficial to them, I will directly match these three values to show substantial cooperation. In exchange, I am holding firm on my highest-priority issues, issueA and issueB, giving me a solid targeted utility of 0.72. The opponent's emotional messages are treated as cheap talk; I am guiding my concessions entirely by their actual bidding behavior.

We are just past the midpoint (t=0.54) of the negotiation to the deadline. To ensure my utility remains high while seeking common ground, I will hold firm on my high-priority issues, Issue A and Issue B. I am conceding on Issue D and Issue E, where my weights are low, by offering values the opponent frequently requests or has recently proposed, like valueE for Issue D and valueB for Issue E. The opponent's message history is entirely emotional appeals, which I will treat as cheap talk while focusing on their actual bid numbers to guide my concessions.

I am maintaining high utility on my critical issues (issueA and issueB) while looking for acceptable tradeoffs on issues where I have lower weight. The opponent has persistently emotionally appealed without making consistent high-value concessions, acting randomly on issueA and issueB. Yet they have repeatedly requested valueE for issueD and valueB for issueE in recent turns; I will concede these lower-weight issues to match their recent bids, whilst holding firm at values A, B, and B on my heavily weighted issues to ensure a strong target utility of 0.80.

The negotiation is past the 50% mark, so I am beginning to present tradeoffs heavily shaped by the opponent's concessions on my lower-weighted issues. I notice the opponent consistently pushes for `valueE` in `issueA` (my highest weight), which I refuse, but they are willing to accept `valueB` on `issueE` and explore various options on `issueB` and `issueC`. To build on this, I am maintaining my high-utility values on `issueA` and `issueB`, conceding to `valueE` on `issueD` and `valueB` on `issueE` reflecting their recent offers, and moderately compromising on `issueC` to keep overall utility above 0.8.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals, which are treated as cheap talk; I am focusing on their actual bidding pattern. They consistently target valueE on issueA, issueD, and issueE (though occasionally accepting/exploring others), and they have started moderating their requests on issueB and issueC. To maintain a strong utility around 0.80 at `t=0.6` while showing willingness to cooperate, I will concede on issueD (valueE) and issueE (valueB) where my weights are lowest, but stand firm on issueA (valueA) and issueB (valueB) where my priorities are highest.

The opponent's numerous emotional appeals contain no verifiable constraints and can be treated as cheap talk. Analyzing their recent bids, they show a strong preference for issueD=valueE, issueE=valueB, and issueA=valueE, while remaining flexible on issueB (having recently conceded to valueA). I will maintain my top values on my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueB), fully concede on my lowest-weight issue (issueD), and offer partial concessions on issueC and issueE to aim for a utility around 0.76 as we enter the mid-to-late phase of the negotiation.

The opponent's emotional appeals are treated as generic negotiation tactics, but their actual offers reveal interesting concessions on my lower-weight issues. In recent rounds, they have offered `issueB: valueA`, `issueC: valueD`, `issueD: valueE`, and `issueE: valueA`. I am constructing a counter-offer that integrates these specific values to test if they can accept an offer heavily composed of their own recent concessions, while I hold firm on `issueA: valueA` to secure a utility of 0.80.

I am holding firm on issueA and issueB as they carry the highest weight for me (0.3 each), while making strategic concessions on issueC, issueD, and issueE. The opponent relies heavily on emotional appeals in their messages, which I consider cheap talk; I am instead extracting signal from their actual bids, which show recent acceptance of valueA on issueE. I will offer a compromise that continues locking in favorable terms for my lower-weight issues while remaining above my mid-game utility target of 0.75.

The opponent's most recent offer gave me valueA on issueB, indicating it may be a low-weight issue for them but it provides me with high utility (0.3). They consistently request valueE on issueA and issueD. To make progress at t=0.68 while maintaining a strong utility to reach an agreement, I will continue conceding on my lower-weighted issues (valueE for issueD, valueA for issueE) and offer a slight concession on issueA (lowering to valueB to signal flexibility), while securing maximum utility on issueB. This maintains my total utility around 0.78. I am disregarding the opponent's emotional appeals as they are non-binding tactics.

The time fraction is 0.7, so I need to maintain a moderately high utility (around 0.75 - 0.80) while moving towards a potential agreement. Looking at the opponent's recent offers, they have demonstrated flexibility on issueB (offering valueA at t=0.68) and issueE (offering valueA at t=0.64, t=0.66, and t=0.70). I will secure valueA for both issueB and issueE while offering concessions on issueC and issueD, aiming for a favorable compromise. The opponent's continuous emotional messages are classic cheap talk, so I will ignore them and rely purely on their offer history.

The current time is 0.72, and the opponent relies heavily on emotional appeals in their messages, which I am treating as cheap talk. Their offers demonstrate a strong desire to maximize their payoffs on issueA while occasionally conceding my highly-weighted issueB and issueE. Accordingly, I will propose a bid with a slight compromise on issueA (valueB) and issueC/D (valueC), while locking in max utility on my important issueB and the often-conceded issueE to target an overall utility around 0.82.

The opponent has made significant concessions in their recent offer, bringing their proposal's utility up to 0.70 by offering valueA on issueB and valueC on multiple other issues. As time advances past 0.70, I will reciprocate their movement by accepting their terms for issueB, issueC, and issueD, and I will concede issueE to valueB, which they frequently proposed between t=0.52 and t=0.62. I am holding my position on my highest-weight issue, issueA, to secure a total utility of 0.80, demonstrating flexibility while safeguarding my core interests.

We are at t=0.76 and the opponent's messages consist of emotional appeals without hard constraints. Looking closely at their bidding history, they proposed `issueA: valueA` and `issueE: valueB` in their most recent offer, and `issueB: valueA` just prior at t=0.74. I will combine these recently proposed values into my next bid, adding `issueD: valueC` which they have repeatedly requested, which protects my highest-weighted issues while directly matching their demonstrated preferences on the others.

The opponent's last offer (A:valueA, B:valueB, C:valueB, D:valueB, E:valueE) yields me an impressively high utility of 0.80, indicating a major concession. Since we are at t=0.78 and still have some time left, I will secure their agreements on issues A, C, D, and E, but push for my top value on issue B. This slight counteroffer targets a utility of 0.86 while demonstrating that we are extremely close to a final deal.

The negotiation is entering the late stage (t=0.8). The opponent recently proposed offers that gave me top value on issues A, C, and D, but demanded significant concessions on B and E. By merging combinations of their `t=0.78` and `t=0.80` proposals (specifically taking their previous acceptance of `issueB: valueB`), I can formulate an offer that appeals to their prior compromises while keeping my utility robustly above 0.80.

The current time fraction is 0.82, so we are approaching the final stages of the negotiation. At t=0.78, the opponent made an offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueB, issueE: valueE) that grants me an excellent utility of 0.80. I will propose this exact offer now, matching their previously demonstrated acceptance threshold while maintaining a highly favorable outcome for my own preferences.
